text:
My opinion: do **not** try to prove EAT by a literal source-local restriction/deformation of \(C_y^0\). The two-cell obstruction is real. I would reformulate so locality is imposed only after passing to a quotient/dual object.

Here is the portfolio I’d try.

1. **Replace the bad term by a quotient bad seminorm.**  
   Define, for the quotient class \([C]\in N_3(R,\partial R)/\ker f_\#\),
   \[
   \beta_z([C])=\inf_{N\in\ker f_\#}\Mass((C+N)\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)
   \]
   or, more flexibly, an infimum over decompositions \(C+N=C_{\rm good}+C_{\rm bad}\) with \(C_{\rm bad}\) carried by \({\mathcal B}_z\). Then prove EAT with \(\beta_z([C_y^0])\) instead of \(\Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\). Since
   \[
   \beta_z([C_y^0])\le \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z),
   \]
   the same plaque Fubini absorption still works. This is the cleanest way to remove the incompatibility between source-local cutting and \(f\)-null quotienting.

2. **Use the dual Hahn–Banach formulation.**  
   View \(\EFill_f(y)\) as a filling norm in the quotient complex. Its dual involves quotient \(2\)-cochains \(\phi\) annihilating \(\ker f_\#\), with \(\|\delta\phi\|\le1\). Try to prove an averaged cochain homotopy identity of the schematic form
   \[
   \phi(Z_y)=K_z(\delta\phi)(Z_y)+\Pi_z(\delta\phi)(C_y^0),
   \]
   where \(K_z\) is controlled by \(R_1E_y+E_y^2/S_1\), and \(\Pi_z(\delta\phi)\) is supported, in quotient seminorm, on \({\mathcal B}_z\). This avoids restricting quotient cochains to nonsaturated bad sets. In a finite cubical model, this becomes a linear-programming/acylcic-carrier problem; prove uniform estimates there, then pass to currents.

3. **Exact Manhattan carrier, not ordinary cubical deformation.**  
   If using cubulations, prescribe homotopies whose tracks are unions of exact coordinate \(2\)-plaques. Declare a cell bad only if the exact track’s \(F\)-image contains \(z\), not if it comes near \(z\). Then
   \[
   \Pr_z(\text{bad})\lesssim R_3R_4/q
   \]
   follows from area, with no thickened-neighborhood failure. The deformation must be a chain map/homotopy globally, so no seam terms appear. But it should act on quotient/dual representatives, not on the raw \(Z_y\), because low-\(G\)-energy flaps cannot be swept at energy cost.

4. **Choose penalized essential fillings.**  
   Instead of arbitrary near-minimizers, minimize
   \[
   \Mass C+\mu q^{-1}\int_Q \beta_z([C])\,dz.
   \]
   In the hard regime the penalty is \(O(\mu R_3R_4/S_3S_4)\Mass C\), so these are still mass near-minimizers. This gives a selection adapted to the bad-set functional and may make the dual comparison cleaner.

5. **Alternative route: weighted \(3\)-dimensional level theorem.**  
   Slice by \(f_1=t\). On \(M_t\), \(g=(f_2,f_3,f_4)\) has \(\|\Lambda^2dg\|\le1\) and weight \(w=|\nabla f_1|^{-1}\). A sufficient replacement is a hard-regime averaged theorem
   \[
   \int_{M_t}w\gtrsim S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4
   \]
   for most \(t\), with an absorbable plaque-bad error. This is false without \(R_3R_4\ll S_3S_4\), but maybe provable by adapting Guth’s \(3\)D boundary estimate while charging escapes through \(x_1\).

6. **Literature/verification target.**  
   The mixed inequality
   \[
   \frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}
   +\frac{R_1\sqrt{R_3R_4}}{S_1\sqrt{S_3S_4}}
   +\frac{\Vol R}{S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4}\gtrsim1
   \]
   is exactly what to look for in Guth’s unpublished 4D rectangle preprint. If found, it bypasses EAT entirely.
